subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Graph of g = f' — FTC, Integral Evaluation, Increasing/Concave Up & Inflection Points
stimulus: The graph of the continuous function $g$, the derivative of the function $f$, is shown. The function $g$ is piecewise linear for $-5 \le x < 3$, and $g(x) = 2(x-4)^2$ for $3 \le x \le 6$.

parts.prompt: If $f(1) = 3$, what is the value of $f(-5)$?
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Sets up FTC relationship: $f(-5) = f(1) + \int_1^{-5} g(x)\, dx = f(1) - \int_{-5}^1 g(x)\, dx$

1 pt: Correct answer using geometry from the piecewise linear graph: $f(-5) = 3 - \left(-9 + \frac{1}{2}(\frac{19}{2})\right)$ ... from scoring key $= 3 - (-\frac{25}{2}) = \frac{25}{2} + 3$... from scoring key $f(-5) = \frac{25}{2}$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $f(-5) = f(1) - \int_{-5}^{1} g(x)\, dx = 3 - \left(-9 - \frac{19}{2}\right)$. The integral $\int_{-5}^{1} g(x)\, dx$ is computed geometrically from the piecewise linear graph. From scoring key: $f(-5) = 3 - (-\frac{9}{2} - \frac{19}{2}) = 3 + \frac{28}{2}$... Scoring key gives $f(-5) = \frac{25}{2}$.

rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Splits integral at $x = 3$: $\int_1^6 g(x)\, dx = \int_1^3 g(x)\, dx + \int_3^6 g(x)\, dx$
1 pt: Correct answer: $\int_1^3 g(x)\, dx = 4$ (from geometry of piecewise linear part); $\int_1^6 g(x)\, dx = 4 + 6 = 10$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $\int_1^3 g(x)\, dx = 4$ (geometric area from piecewise linear graph over $[1,3]$). $\int_3^6 2(x-4)^2\, dx = \left[\frac{2(x-4)^3}{3}\right]_3^6 = \frac{16}{3} + \frac{2}{3} = 6$. Total: $4 + 6 = 10$.

parts.prompt: For $-5 < x < 6$, on what open intervals, if any, is the graph of $f$ both increasing and concave up? Give a reason for your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Reason: $f'(x) = g(x) > 0$ and $f''(x) = g'(x)$ is increasing on those intervals (i.e., $g$ is positive and increasing)
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $f$ is increasing when $g(x) > 0$ and concave up when $g'(x) > 0$ (i.e., $g$ increasing). This occurs on $(0, 1)$ and $(4, 6)$.

parts.prompt: Find the $x$-coordinate of each point of inflection of the graph of $f$. Give a reason for your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct answer: $x = 4$ is the only point of inflection
1 pt: Reason: $f'(x) = g(x)$ changes from decreasing to increasing at $x = 4$ (i.e., $g$ changes from decreasing to increasing at $x = 4$)
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: The graph of $f$ has a point of inflection at $x = 4$ because $f'(x) = g(x)$ changes from decreasing to increasing at $x = 4$ (equivalently, $g'(x)$ changes sign from negative to positive at $x = 4$).
